<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Analyze level 3 layout
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== ALL NODES ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:

    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]

print("\n=== ADJACENCY ===")
for edge in seg['adjacency_list']:
    print(f"  {edge[0]} - {edge[1]}")
</parameter>
</function>

Looking at the segmentation, this is a completely different level. Let me understand the layout:
- Pink bar at top (rows 0, cols 0-63)
- Large charcoal background (rows 1-63, cols 0-63) containing everything
- Gray area (rows 6-55, cols 6-57) - the main play area
- Several black vertical bars at the bottom
- Blue blocks at the bottom (rows 56-57)
- White regions at the bottom
- Some N (light green) and c (charcoal) objects
- Some Y (yellow) and p (purple) objects
Let me look at the bottom row more carefully. The blue blocks are at rows 56-57, at cols:
- 12-13 (id=23)
- 16-17 (id=24)

The white regions are:

- id=19: rows 52-57, cols 38-47
- id=21: rows 54-57, cols 6-13

There are also black vertical bars:

And there are some small objects:

- Y (yellow) at rows 29-29, cols 52-54 (id=4)
- p (purple) at rows 39-39, cols 36-37 (id=9)

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>

# Click leftmost blue block to probe
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click leftmost blue to see effect')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 57, 'col': 13}])

print("Result:", last_action_result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\n=== KEY OBJECTS ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    b = n['boundary']
    rows = [p[0] for p in b]
    cols = [p[1] for p in b]
    if n['color'] in ('W', 'b', 'N', 'c', 'Y', 'p') and n['pixels'] < 500:
        print(f"  {n['color']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)} cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)} pixels={n['pixels']}")

if last_action_result.get('level_completed') or last_action_result.get('done'):
    print("LEVEL COMPLETED!")
elif last_action_result.get('game_over'):
    print("Game over")
else:
    print("Level active")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
